fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a small utility that can take the raw text output from whatever disk-inspection tool a given OS uses and turn it into a clean, consistent data structure. Right now every platform does its own thing and it's a mess. The idea is basically the same approach we used for that device-normalization work a while back — same kind of key/value block parsing logic.

The tool should handle two main things: parsing raw text into structured records, and knowing which inspection routine to kick off depending on what OS we're running on. If someone asks for a platform we don't support, it should fail gracefully with something machine-readable, not a Python stack trace or whatever.

One tricky bit: some of the raw output from certain tools wraps values in quotes, and some has trailing commas, so the parser needs to clean those up. Also lines that don't have a proper value — or are just bare text with no separator — should still be captured, just with a null marker.

The whole thing needs a test runner that reads case files from a directory and dumps each result to a separate output file for comparison. Should be invokable with a simple bash command. Keep the parsing logic totally separate from any stdin/stdout wiring — we want to be able to unit-test the core without any I/O gymnastics.

Quick follow-up from the questions that came in: if the input is absent, an empty string, or whitespace-only, the parser should return the empty-object sentinel `{}` and not an empty array `[]`. That’s intentional so we can tell “nothing to report” apart from “a list with no items.” For that situation, the JSON output is exactly `{}
`.

Also, the raw text should be treated as records separated by blank lines, and then each non-blank line inside a record is a `key: value` pair. If there are trailing blank lines after the last record, just ignore them — they should not create an extra empty record. And we do need to preserve order exactly as it showed up, both for the records themselves and for the keys inside each record.

On cleanup rules, if a parsed value ends with a single trailing comma, strip that one comma and leave any internal commas alone. So `foo,bar,baz,` should become `foo,bar,baz`. Same idea with quoted values: if the raw inspection output gives us something like `device: "/dev/sda"`, strip the surrounding double-quotes so the stored value is just the literal content without the quotation marks. That should apply the same way everywhere.

One other thing to be explicit about: there are two different ways a key should map to null. If there’s a colon but nothing after it, like `hello:`, that means key `hello`, value null. And if it’s just a bare line with no colon at all, like `hello`, that also means key `hello`, value null. In that second case, the full line text is the key verbatim, including any embedded spaces.

So really this is just locking in the `key: value` block parsing logic described in Feature 1 of start.md, specifically the record-splitting, null-value, trailing-comma, and quote-stripping rules that form the parser's contract.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The toolkit recognizes at least `linux` and `win32` as valid platform identifiers for the `list` action. Any other platform name triggers an immediate unsupported-platform failure before any parsing is attempted.

trigger_keywords: machine-readable error when platform not found
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
When the platform is not supported, the output must be exactly two lines: `error=unsupported_platform
` followed by `platform=<name>
` where `<name>` is the offending platform string. No stack traces, exception messages, or JSON wrapping should appear.

trigger_keywords: how to run the test suite
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The test harness is invoked with `bash rcb_tests/test.sh`. It accepts an optional `--cases-dir <subdir>` argument (defaulting to `public_test_cases`). For each case it writes only the raw stdout of the program under test to `rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/<filename_stem>@<zero_padded_case_index>.txt`. Output directories are namespaced by cases-dir so different runs never overwrite each other.

The execution adapter reads a single JSON object from stdin. The `action` field selects behavior: `parse` uses the `input` field as raw text; `list` uses the `platform` and `output` fields. Structured results are written as canonical single-line JSON followed by `
`. The core parsing/enumeration logic must be fully decoupled from this I/O layer.

The codebase must be organized across multiple files reflecting distinct responsibilities: a core parser module, a platform enumeration/routing module, and a separate I/O adapter entry point. A single-file 'god file' approach is explicitly prohibited. A directory structure with `src/` and `tests/` or equivalent separation is expected.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which action strings does the adapter support
what are the possible action inputs
tell me all the action names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'parse', 'list'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: same kind of key/value block parsing logic
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The parse() function responsible for splitting raw text on blank lines into ordered dicts, handling the four edge cases: empty input → `{}`, trailing comma stripping, null for missing/keyless values, and surrounding-quote removal

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect of the requirement. Please check the existing test case files under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` and the feature descriptions in `start.md` for the precise behavioral contract, then implement accordingly and ask me if a specific behavior is still unclear.

feature1_1_empty_input.json.description: Parsing an input that is absent, empty, or composed only of whitespace yields the empty-collection sentinel rather than a list of records. The output is the textual empty-object form.

feature1_2_records.json.description: Parsing a textual block of one or more records, where records are separated by a blank line and each record is a set of `key: value` lines, produces an ordered list of key/value maps. Record order and per-record key order follow the input; records need not share the same keys, and any blank lines trailing the final record are ignored.

input.input:
device: /dev/disk1
description: Macintosh HD
size: 249.8 GB
mountpoint: /

device: /dev/disk1
description: Macintosh HD
size: 249.8 GB
mountpoint: /

device: /dev/disk2
description: elementary OS
size: 15.7 GB
mountpoint: /Volumes/Elementary

feature1_3_trailing_commas.json.description: When a value ends with one trailing comma, that comma is stripped from the parsed value while internal commas are preserved.

feature1_4_null_values.json.description: A line that declares a key with no value, or a bare line with no `key: value` separator at all, parses to that key mapped to the null sentinel. The remaining text becomes the key verbatim, including embedded spaces.

feature2_1_platform_enumeration.json.description: Enumerating devices for a recognized operating-system platform routes to that platform's collection step, captures its raw textual output, and returns the parsed list of device records. The same parsing contract applies regardless of platform; only the supplied raw output differs.

input.output:
device: "/dev/sda"
description: "My drive"
size: "15 GB"
mountpoint: "/mnt/drive"

device: "\\\\.\\PHYSICALDRIVE1"
description: "My drive"
size: "15 GB"
mountpoint: "D:"

feature2_2_unsupported_platform.json.description: Requesting enumeration for a platform the toolkit does not support fails up front with a neutral, language-independent error category and the offending platform name carried in its own field.
